food_subgroup: Legume-based dishes / Lentil soups and curries

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambar or dhal sambar; lentil soup (FOODON_03311737) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for sambar; A00LC (legume-based soups and stews) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dhal sambar or sambar as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dhal sambar in a Singapore banana leaf meal is approximately 200 g, served as a side soup or poured over rice.

nutrient_source.title: Sambar (lentil-based vegetable stew) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports sambar as a standard South Indian lentil-vegetable stew. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for sambar prepared with toor dal, tamarind, tomatoes, and mixed vegetables. This is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. Values cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of lentil-based dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for sambar using split pigeon pea (toor dal) GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dhal sambar exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 41 (low) based on the GI of cooked toor dal / split pigeon peas (GI ~29–41, University of Sydney GI Database) and the diluting effect of tamarind broth and vegetables. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (41 × 6.5 × 2) / 100 = 5.3 g. This is consistent with the low-GI classification of legume-based soups.

hypertension.guidance: Dhal sambar contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (420 mg per 200 g serving), which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as restaurant versions may contain additional salt in the tempering oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dhal sambar has a low estimated glycemic index (~41) and provides dietary fibre (~1.6 g per 100 g) from toor dal, which slows glucose absorption and supports postprandial glycaemic control, making it a suitable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based protein, and soluble fibre from lentils
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dhal sambar is low in saturated fat (0.3 g per 100 g) and provides plant-based protein and soluble fibre from toor dal, which are associated with improved LDL cholesterol levels; it is a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dhal sambar is a South Indian lentil-based vegetable stew that is a staple of South Indian cuisine and widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants, particularly in banana leaf rice meals. It is prepared by simmering cooked toor dal (split pigeon peas) with tamarind water, tomatoes, onions, drumstick (moringa pods), and mixed vegetables, seasoned with sambar powder — a spice blend containing coriander, cumin, black pepper, dried red chillies, and curry leaves — and finished with a tempering (tadka) of mustard seeds, dried red chillies, and curry leaves in oil. The result is a thin, tangy, mildly spicy soup with a complex layered flavour profile: sour from tamarind, earthy from lentils, and aromatic from the spice tempering. Nutritionally, dhal sambar is low in calories (~48 kcal per 100 g), moderate in plant-based protein (~2.8 g), low in fat (~1.5 g), and provides dietary fibre (~1.6 g) from lentils. Its low glycemic index (~41) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate (~210 mg per 100 g) and should be monitored by those with hypertension. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore, dhal sambar is commonly served alongside rice, idli, dosa, or roti prata as a dipping or pouring sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), which is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. No exact entry for 'dhal sambar' as served in Singapore exists; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN sambar entry and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate based on toor dal GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact Singapore restaurant-specific measurement is available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for sambar, the closest approved-source match. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as sambar is prepared from plant-based ingredients with no industrial trans fats; confirmed zero by formulation. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products; naturally zero. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. 7. GI is an analogous estimate of 41 (low) based on toor dal / split pigeon pea GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for dhal sambar exists. 8. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (41 × 6.5 × 2) / 100 = 5.3 g. 9. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as lentils are classified under protein/meat alternatives in Singapore dietary classification systems. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact ontology term for sambar exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2. 11. The cuisine tag in the input lists 'Malaysian' but the dish is South Indian in origin; cuisine field corrected to 'Indian / South Indian' with Singapore serving context noted.
